coredata.dc:title: Returnable MoS2@carbon nitride nanotube composite hollow spheres drive photo-self-Fenton-PMS system for synergistic catalytic and photocatalytic tetracycline degradation
coredata.dc:creator: An, Beiya
Liu, Jialiang
Zhu, Benjie
Liu, Fang
Jiang, Guofei
Duan, Xiaoguang
Wang, Yongqiang
Sun, Juan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we synthesized hollow sphere composites of MoS2@carbon nitride nanotube (MoS2@TCN-S) for in-situ formation of H2O2 under visible light. The photo-self-Fenton system was further coupled with peroxymonosulfate (P-SF-PMS) for promoted reactive oxygen species generation and tetracycline degradation, so that the P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S) system can remove 94.3 % TC within 120 min, and the removal efficiencies after 15 cycles decrease by less than 5 %. The P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S) system also shows excellent degradation performance for actual wastewater, with COD removal rates of domestic sewage and secondary sedimentation tank effluent reaching 64.6 % and 58.7 % within 120 min. Quenching experiment and ESR results reveal the synergy of PMS and P-SF system, which was the key to improving the degradability and pH (3–9) adaptability of the P-SF-PMS system. Under alkaline conditions, PMS and H2O2 generated on MoS2@TCN-S activate each other and form 1O2 and OH, which solves the problem of poor degradation of P-SF system under alkaline conditions. Under acidic and neutral conditions, O2 – becomes the main ROS, the activation of PMS by O2 – and the synergistic effect of PMS and H2O2 make the P-SF-PMS system get rid of the dependence on H+. In addition, direct Z-scheme heterojunction enables MoS2@TCN-S to exhibit faster electron transfer ability, faster H2O2 generation ability, stronger adsorption capacity for PMS, and lower activation barrier for *HSO5, which also enhances the synergistic effect between PMS and P-SF system. In this study, a new type of multi-functional water purification material is proposed, and the synergism of PMS and P-SF system is revealed.

(a) XPS survey scan spectra of the prepared catalysts; high resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) S 2p and (f) Mo 3d of the prepared catalysts.

Diagrams of the prepared catalyst: (a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) UV–visible absorption spectra, (c) XPS-VB spectrum, (d) Mott-Schottky spectrum, (e) differential charge density diagram (the green and purple isosurfaces represent the regions of net electron accumulation and deficiency, and the isosurface value is 0.002 electron/Å3), (f) band gap structures (g) PL spectrum, (h) EIS spectra and (i) I-T photocurrent response spectra.

Performance of (a) TCN-S and MoS2@TCN-S3, (b) MoS2@TCN-S1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 for in-situ formation of H2O2 in P-SF system; (c) degradation performance of TC by P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S) system; (d) the shedding condition of MoS2@TCN-S after reaction in P-SF system; (e) degradation performance and (f) degradation Kinetics fit plots of TC under different reaction conditions (c(TC) = 10 mg/L, c(PMS) = 0.75 mM, c(MoS2@TCN) = 1 g/L, pH = 7); (g) cycle experiment of degradation of TC by P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system.

Adsorption model of (a-c) TCN and (d-f) MoS2@TCN for PMS; the differential charge density of PMS adsorbed on the surface of (g) TCN and (h) MoS2@TCN (green and purple equivalent surfaces represent net electron accumulation and depletion, respectively, and the equivalent face value is 0.02 electron·Å−3); (i) the free energy of PMS activated by MoS2@TCN, TCN and MoS2.

(a) Quenching experiment of H2O2 generated by P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system; (b) H2O2 production of P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system under different pH conditions; degradation of TC by (c) P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system and (e) P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system under different pH conditions; the contribution of ROS in the process of TC degradation by (d) P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system and (f) P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system; ESR analysis of (g) O2 –, (h) 1O2 and (i) OH in different system.
(a) Chemical structure, (b) HOMO, (c) LUMO and (d) Fukui index of TC; (e) degradation pathway of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system.

Effects of (a) interference anions (10 mM) and (b) interference cations (10 mM) on the degradation of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system; 3D fluorescence spectrum in degradation process of (c) DS, (d) DS-SE and (e) COD photocatalytic degradation curve of DS and DS-SE by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system; (f) Degradation of TC by different systems under natural light.
Photocatalytic degradation mechanism of TC in P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system.

head.ce:title: Returnable MoS2@carbon nitride nanotube composite hollow spheres drive photo-self-Fenton-PMS system for synergistic catalytic and photocatalytic tetracycline degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we synthesized hollow sphere composites of MoS2@carbon nitride nanotube (MoS2@TCN-S) for in-situ formation of H2O2 under visible light. The photo-self-Fenton system was further coupled with peroxymonosulfate (P-SF-PMS) for promoted reactive oxygen species generation and tetracycline degradation, so that the P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S) system can remove 94.3 % TC within 120 min, and the removal efficiencies after 15 cycles decrease by less than 5 %. The P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S) system also shows excellent degradation performance for actual wastewater, with COD removal rates of domestic sewage and secondary sedimentation tank effluent reaching 64.6 % and 58.7 % within 120 min. Quenching experiment and ESR results reveal the synergy of PMS and P-SF system, which was the key to improving the degradability and pH (3–9) adaptability of the P-SF-PMS system. Under alkaline conditions, PMS and H2O2 generated on MoS2@TCN-S activate each other and form 1O2 and OH, which solves the problem of poor degradation of P-SF system under alkaline conditions. Under acidic and neutral conditions, O2 – becomes the main ROS, the activation of PMS by O2 – and the synergistic effect of PMS and H2O2 make the P-SF-PMS system get rid of the dependence on H+. In addition, direct Z-scheme heterojunction enables MoS2@TCN-S to exhibit faster electron transfer ability, faster H2O2 generation ability, stronger adsorption capacity for PMS, and lower activation barrier for *HSO5, which also enhances the synergistic effect between PMS and P-SF system. In this study, a new type of multi-functional water purification material is proposed, and the synergism of PMS and P-SF system is revealed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In the process of industrialization and urbanization, more and more refractory organic wastewater with complex components and high toxicity are discharged into the environmental environment, which poses a potential risk to the environment and human health [1,2]. Antibiotics are common emerging contaminants in water. It is also one of the most urgent and refractory organic pollutants in the global natural water environment [3,4]. Tetracycline (TC) is a typical antibiotic, which is widely used in the prevention and treatment of human and animal diseases [5]. However, only a small portion of TC can be absorbed by the body, while the majority (about 75 %) will be released into the environment [6], destroying the existing ecological balance and adversely affecting human society [7]. Therefore, it is particularly important to explore the methods to effectively remove TC from the water environment.

Compared with traditional physical, chemical and biological methods, advanced oxidation processes (AOP) have an efficient removal effect on refractory organic pollutants such as antibiotics, and is a promising method for water treatment [8]. The AOP based on peroxymonosulfate (PMS) is a water treatment technology which can quickly and effectively remove organic pollutants from water over a wide pH range [9]. On the one hand, PMS can effectively capture electrons (e−) and promote the transfer and separation of photogenerated charges [10]. On the other hand, the sulfate radical produced by PMS activation has a high oxidation potential (2.7 ∼ 3.1 V), selectivity and long half-life (30 ∼ 40 μs), which is conducive to the destruction of pollutant structures [11,12]. There are many ways to activate PMS, including photocatalysis, heat treatment, electrocatalysis, transition metal dissociation catalysis, semiconductors, ultrasonic radiation, and carbon-based catalyst catalysis [13,14]. Among them, photocatalytic activation of PMS photocatalysis is widely used because of its mild reaction conditions and green environmental protection. The existence of PMS can greatly improve the degradation performance of the photocatalytic system. Thus, photocatalytic activation of PMS technology has become an effective strategy for the removal of organic pollutants.

Photo-Fenton technology in AOP is also considered as an effective and convenient water purification strategy with good degradation performance, simple operation and high mineralization rate [15]. However, the photo-Fenton is over-dependent on Fe2+ and H2O2, resulting in high ferrous salt and H2O2 input in the effluent and generation of iron-containing sludge, which increase the operating cost. In addition, the process can only work in a narrow range of pH (pH < 3), which limits the conditions for the operation of the process [16,17]. Interestingly, the photocatalytic activation of PMS is hardly affected by pH value, so some researchers couple the photo-Fenton process with the photocatalytic activation of PMS to form a photo-Fenton coupled PMS double oxidation system that can work in a wide range of pH. Through the interaction of these two processes, the removal efficiency of pollutants can be significantly improved [18,19]. Hu et al. [20] constructed a BFO (mullite bismuth ferrites)/H2O2/PMS system for switchable catalysis and hybrid reaction under visible light irradiation. Compared with BFO/PMS, visible light/BFO/H2O2 or other systems, the hybrid reaction showed the best pollutant degradation efficiency in environmental purification. Zhou et al. [21] used MnO2/UIO-66 instead of iron-based catalyst to activate PMS and H2O2 to degrade oxytetracycline (OTC) under ultraviolet light. The results showed that the degradation of OTC in PMS/H2O2 system is promoted.

Iron-free photo-self-Fenton extended on the basis of photo-Fenton can generate H2O2 in situ under the action of visible light and catalyst to promote the degradation of pollutants. There is no need to add additional H2O2 and avoids the existence of Fe2+ [22], which reduces the reaction cost and the difficulty of treatment. Therefore, using photo-self-Fenton instead of photo-Fenton coupled with PMS to form photo-self-Fenton/PMS double oxidation system can not only avoid the exogenous addition of H2O2 and the generation of iron mud, but also effectively improve the photocatalytic degradation performance of the system. Unfortunately, at present, the research on photo-self-Fenton coupled PMS system is still relatively few, especially the synergistic effect between photo-self-Fenton system and PMS has not been deeply studied. Therefore, this study will focus on the synergistic mechanism of photo-self-Fenton coupled PMS system.

In order to realize the construction of photo-self-Fenton/PMS system, the preparation of efficient photocatalyst is particularly important. At present, researchers have developed many photocatalytic materials, such as TiO2 [23], g-C3N4 [24], CoFe2O4 [25], Bi2O3 [26], ZnO [13] and so on. Among them, graphite phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a kind of metal-free photocatalyst with stable properties, strong visible light and low cost. it is widely used in photocatalytic self-produced H2O2 and photocatalytic activation of PMS [27,28]. Unfortunately, g-C3N4 has the problems of small specific surface area (SSA) and easy recombination of photogenerated electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs, which limits the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 and the efficiency of H2O2 generation [29]. A variety of modifications are carried out to optimize the properties of g-C3N4, such as morphology control [30], element doping [31], heterostructure construction [32,33]. MoS2 is an excellent co-catalyst, which has a large SSA and narrow band gap (Eg≈1.9 eV) [34]. Importantly, MoS2 can produce more photogenerated-e−, promote the production of H2O2 and its transformation to hydroxyl radical (OH), and improve the degradation performance of pollutants [35–37]. Therefore, the heterojunction coupling of MoS2 and g-C3N4 can increase the active sites of the catalyst to promote the transfer of photogenerated-e−.

In this study, MoS2@carbon nitride nanotube (TCN) photocatalyst was loaded on the surface of hollow sphere carrier by impregnation method to prepare MoS2@TCN composite hollow spheres (MoS2@TCN-S). The Z-scheme heterostructure between MoS2 and TCN made MoS2@TCN-S stronger than TCN-S in photocatalytic self-production of H2O2 and degradation of TC, DFT calculation also showed that MoS2@TCN-S has stronger photocatalytic ability to activate PMS than TCN-S. MoS2@TCN-S could successfully drive photo-self-Fenton coupled PMS (P-SF-PMS) multifunctional oxidation system to degrade TC. The mutual activation mechanism of reactive oxygen species (ROS, such as O2 –, OH, etc.) between P-SF system and PMS was the key for MoS2@TCN-S to drive P-SF-PMS system to degrade TC. Thanks to the synergistic effect between P-SF system and PMS, P-SF-PMS system overcame the limitation of alkaline conditions. In this study, DFT calculation, Fukui function and LC-MS test were used to analyze the degradation pathway of TC in P-SF-PMS system. Cyclic experiments and degradation experiments under natural light showed that MoS2@TCN-S also has excellent cyclic stability and natural light response. In summary, this study provided a new green and effective approach for sustainable water decontamination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MoS2@carbon nitride nanotube composite photocatalytic hollow spheres
ce:section.ce:para: MoS2@carbon nitride nanotube (MoS2@TCN) composite photocatalytic hollow spheres were prepared by impregnation method. Firstly, waterborne polyurethane (WPU), deionized water and MoS2@TCN were mixed uniformly at the ratio of 1:1:240, 1:3:480, 1:5:720, 1:7:960 and 1:9:1200 (the unit of WPU and deionized water was “mL”, the unit of MoS2@TCN was “mg”, the preparation method of MoS2@TCN was shown in Supplementary Materials Text S1,2). After ultrasonic stirring, the catalyst mixture solution was obtained. Reduced graphene oxide (r-GO) was added to the catalyst mixture solution at the ratio of m (MoS2@TCN):m(r-GO) = 25:1 to improve the conductivity of the spherical catalyst and accelerate the transport of photogenerated carriers. A certain amount of rigid hollow sphere carrier with a diameter of 3–5 mm was added to the catalyst mixture, stirred evenly, then removed and dried. After drying, the hollow spheres were added to the mixed solution again, and after repeated impregnation for three times, MoS2@TCN composite photocatalytic hollow spheres were obtained, which were recorded as MoS2@TCN-SX (X = 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5, indicating the different mixing ratios of WPU, deionized water and MoS2@TCN). After calculation, the amount of MoS2@TCN contained in 1 g MoS2@TCN-S was about 50 mg. Fig. S1 and S2 show the preparation circuit diagram of MoS2@TCN-S. If the MoS2@TCN in the above steps was replaced by the carbon nitride nanotube (TCN, the preparation method of TCN was shown in Text S1), the TCN photocatalytic hollow spheres were obtained, which were named TCN-S.

The structure and properties of MoS2@TCN-S hollow spheres were characterized by field emission electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), X-ray fluorescence spectrometer (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR), UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectrometer (UV–Vis DRS), photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer, photoelectric performance test and electron spin resonance (ESR). The detailed description of the characterization of the catalyst and the calculation method of density functional theory (DFT) were listed in Text S3,4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance test of P-SF-PMS system for degradation of TC
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-self-Fenton coupled PMS (P-SF-PMS) multifunctional oxidation system consists of a double-layer glass reactor, light source (a 300 W xenon lamp with a cut-off filter of λ < 420 nm), the catalysts and PMS. During the reaction, the reaction temperature was maintained at 25 °C by a circulating cooling water device and the solution was fully stirred by a magnetic agitator (Fig. S3). 50 mL pollutant solution (10 mg/L) and 1 g photocatalyst were added into the reactor and carry out dark reaction on the solution for 60 min to reach the adsorption equilibrium. Then add PMS (0.75 mM, KHSO5·0.5 KHSO4·0.5 K2SO4) to the reactor and turn on the xenon lamp to irradiate 120 min. 5 mL solution was sampled and filtered at regular intervals during the reaction, the change of pollutant concentration was determined by ultraviolet spectrophotometer. After determination, the sample was poured back into the reactor to ensure that the concentration of the catalyst in the solution remained unchanged. After the reaction, the yield of H2O2 in the system was determined by potassium titanium oxalate method. The information of intermediate products was monitored by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), and the specific pollutant and H2O2 detection method was described in Text S5. The photo-self-Fenton (P-SF) system consists of a double glass reactor, light source and catalysts, and all other conditions were the same as those of the P-SF-PMS system except that there was no need to add PMS. All experiments used pure deionized water with dissolved oxygen content of about 6 ∼ 8 mg/L. At the end of the reaction, the catalysts were washed with deionized water and dried, and then continued to use.

Fig. 1 a shows the prepared MoS2@TCN-S3. All the MoS2@TCN-S3 are gray spherical structure, their size and shape are relatively uniform. The morphology and structure of the prepared catalyst are observed by SEM, TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The complete tubular structure can be observed in TCN-S (Fig. 1b and e), which is consistent with the shape of TCN (Fig. S4a), indicating that the hollow sphere catalyst prepared by impregnation method will not destroy the morphology and structure of the original powder catalyst. The diameter of carbon nitride nanotubes in TCN-S ranges from 61 to 69 nm, this small nanotube structure can increase the specific surface area of the material, provide more active sites and promote the transfer of photogenerated carriers [33]. Fig. 1c, f shows the SEM and TEM images of MoS2. MoS2 presents a “nano-flower” structure composed of nano-flakes. Similar to MoS2@TCN (Fig. S4b), SEM and TEM images of MoS2@TCN-S3 clearly show that MoS2 is successfully loaded onto TCN (Fig. 1d, g). The TEM image of MoS2@TCN-S3 shows that the diameter of TCN nanotubes is still in the ranges from 61 to 69 nm, and the composite with MoS2 does not change its morphology and structure. The lattice fringes with spacing of 0.61 nm can be clearly seen in the high-resolution HRTEM of MoS2@TCN-S3 (Fig. 1h), which corresponds to the (001) crystal plane of MoS2 crystal [38]. There is a clear boundary between TCN and MoS2 (Fig. 1h, yellow dotted line), which proves the successful recombination of the two materials and the formation of tight heterojunction.

The elemental composition and valence structure of the catalysts are analyzed by XPS. Fig. 2 a shows that MoS2@TCN-S3 is composed of six elements: C, N, O, Cl, S and Mo, indicating that MoS2 and TCN have successfully combined. Fig. 2b shows the high-resolution orbital binding energy spectrum of C1s. where the peaks at 284.8, 286.2 and 288.2 eV correspond to the C = C bond of graphite carbon atom, the C-O bond and the sp2 hybrid carbon in N-C = N, respectively [39]. There are four peaks in the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c), among which the peaks at 398.7, 399.5 and 401.2 eV belong to sp2-hybridized nitrogen (N-C = N), tertiary nitrogen (N-(C)3) and amino nitrogen (C-N-H) [40]. The peak at 404.2 eV can be attributed to π*→π excitation. The peak of O 2p at 532.3 eV belongs to C-OH (Fig. 2d), the existence of this peak may be due to the incorporation of O atoms in the hydrothermal and calcination process of TCN-S [41]. In the high-resolution orbital binding energy spectrum of S 2p, the peaks of MoS2@TCN-S3 at 161.3 and 162.5 eV belong to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of S2− (Fig. 2e), which proves the existence of MoS2 in MoS2@TCN-S3. The two peaks of Mo 3d at 228.6 and 231.8 eV belong to Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 of Mo4+, and the peaks at 225.8 eV belong to S 2p (Fig. 2f) [42], the existence of these three peaks once again proves the interfacial interaction between MoS2 and TCN.

Wide-angle XRD analysis show that the composite of MoS2 and TCN is successful, and loading MoS2@TCN on the surface of the spherical carrier will not change its original crystal structure. FT-IR spectrum shows that both MoS2@TCN powder and MoS2@TCN-S3 hollow spheres retain the molecular structure of TCN. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm and BJH pore size distribution curve show that MoS2@TCN-S3 has larger specific surface area and pore size than TCN-S, which is beneficial to improve the contact probability of MoS2@TCN-S3 with PMS and pollutants, and to improve the photoactivation rate of PMS and the degradation rate of pollutants. Text S6 shows the detailed analysis of wide-angle XRD, FT-IR spectrum, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distribution curve.

The energy band structure of the catalyst is analyzed by UV–Vis DRS, XPS-valence band (XPS-VB) spectra and Mott-Schottky spectra to further understand the charge transfer process. Fig. 3 a shows that MoS2 shows full-spectrum absorption characteristics, while TCN-S and MoS2@TCN-S3 show typical semiconductor absorption characteristics. After recombination with MoS2, MoS2@TCN-S3 shows stronger and wider visible light absorption capacity, and the edge of the optical absorption band is red-shifted from 479 nm of TCN-S to 486 nm of MoS2@TCN-S3, which indicates that MoS2@TCN-S3 can leverage more photon energies. The band gap (Eg) of the catalyst is calculated by the converted Kubelka-Munk function [43]. The results show that the band gap of TCN is 2.52 eV (Fig. 3b). The XPS-VB spectrum shows that the valence band potential (EVB) of TCN is 1.57 V, and EVB of MoS2 is 0.20 V (Fig. 3c). The conduction band potential (ECB) of MoS2 is determined to be −1.18 V by Mott-Schottky spectrum (Fig. 3d, pH = 6.8, electrode potential has been converted).

The differential charge density diagram of MoS2@TCN shows that there is a net electron loss region on the TCN surface and an obvious net electron accumulation region on the MoS2 surface (Fig. 3e), indicating that e− is mainly transferred from the TCN surface to the MoS2 surface. According to the in-situ irradiation XPS spectrum (Fig. S5), compared with the dark condition, the characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s and S2p belonging to TCN on MoS2@TCN-S3 shift to higher binding energy under visible light irradiation, and the characteristic peaks of S2p and Mo3d belonging to MoS2 shift to lower binding energy, which once again indicates that the e− on the surface of MoS2@TCN-S3 can be transferred from TCN to MoS2 [41,44,45]. It is worth noting that the intensity of the characteristic peak of S2p belonging to TCN on MoS2@TCN-S3 increases significantly under light conditions (Fig. S5c), indicating the formation of chemical bonds between TCN and MoS2, which promotes the e− transfer in the built-in electric field. The differential charge density diagram and in-situ irradiation XPS spectra jointly prove that the photogenerated-e− are transferred from TCN to MoS2. Because the ECB of MoS2 (-1.18 V) is more negative than that of TCN (-0.95 V), according to the type II heterojunction theory, photogenerated e− will transfer from the CB of MoS2 to the CB of TCN [46], which is inconsistent with the above results, so the heterojunction between MoS2 and TCN does not belong to type II heterojunction. According to energy band theory and thermodynamic theory, it can be concluded that direct Z-scheme heterostructure is formed between TCN and MoS2 (Fig. 3f) [47]. Finally, MoS2@TCN-S3 relies on the accumulation of photogenerated-e− at the conduction band (CB) of MoS2 for reduction reaction, and on the accumulation of h+ at the valence band (VB) of TCN for oxidation reaction [48].

The PL spectra of the catalyst at 320 nm excitation wavelength are measured to analyze the separation efficiency of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs. As shown in Fig. 3g, TCN-S has the highest peak, and the separation rate of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs on its surface is low. The emission peak intensity of MoS2@TCN-S3 is much lower than that of TCN-S, indicating that the direct Z-scheme heterojunction formed by TCN and MoS2 can effectively separate photogenerated e−-h+ pairs and promote the transfer of photogenerated carriers [49]. The separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers in the catalyst samples are further analyzed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent response. Fig. 3h depicts that MoS2@TCN-S3 has the smallest arc radius of Nyquist impedance, indicating obviously reduced transfer resistance of photogenerated carriers after the introduction of MoS2 [33]. The transient photocurrent response curve shows that several catalysts show reversible photocurrent response in multiple switching cycles (Fig. 3i), which indicates that the photogenerated-e− will be transferred to the back contact surface of the catalyst under light irradiation to form photocurrent [50]. The photocurrent density of MoS2@TCN-S3 is much higher than that of TCN-S, indicating that the direct Z-scheme heterojunction formed by the combination of the two materials effectively reduces the recombination efficiency of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of P-SF-PMS system for degradation of TC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the ability of P-SF system to generate H2O2 in situ and its degradation performance, the H2O2 yield of MoS2@TCN-S3 and the performance of TC degradation by MoS2@TCN-S3 in the system when pH is 7 is studied. In Fig. 4 a, it can be seen that the H2O2 yield of P-SF(TCN-S) and P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system is 8.1 and 28.0 μmol·g−1·h−1 respectively, the H2O2 yield of MoS2@TCN-S3 is 3.4 times higher than that of TCN-S. It shows that MoS2@TCN-S3 is more conducive to the formation of H2O2. The degradation rates of TC by P-SF(TCN-S) and P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) are 61.7 % and 78.1 % (Fig. S6). When TCN and MoS2 are compounded, a tight direct Z-scheme heterojunction will be formed between them, allowing the material to have faster electron transport capacity, which promote in situ generation of H2O2 and accelerate the drive of the P-SF system.

The H2O2 yields of P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S1, 2, 3, 4 and 5) are 13.4, 21.4, 28.0, 29.9 and 31.6 μmol·g−1·h−1 (Fig. 4b), and the degradation rates of TC by P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S1, 2, 3, 4 and 5) are 42.2 %, 63.2 %, 78.1 %, 82.8 % and 91.8 %, respectively (Fig. 4c). WPU will cover the active site of the catalyst, so the lower the proportion of WPU in MoS2@TCN-S is more conducive to its degradation. The degradation of TC in the 120 min by P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S1, 2, 3, 4 and 5) accorded with the quasi-first-order reaction kinetic model (Fig. S7). The kinetic constant (k) increases with the increase of the proportion of MoS2@TCN in MoS2@TCN-S, which is consistent with the results of TC degradation. Unfortunately, when the proportion of WPU in MoS2@TCN-S is low, the mechanical strength of MoS2@TCN-S will be weakened. Fig. 4d shows the shedding status of MoS2@TCN-S1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 after 120 min reaction in P-SF system. MoS2@TCN-S1, 2 and 3 do not fall off after the reaction, and the pollutant solution remains clear and transparent, indicating that MoS2@TCN-S1, 2 and 3 have good mechanical strength. Obvious material shedding can be observed in the reactor after MoS2@TCN-S4 reaction, indicating that the mechanical strength of MoS2@TCN-S4 has been weakened. After MoS2@TCN-S5 reaction, the solution in the reactor becomes very turbid, and a large number of catalysts on the surface of MoS2@TCN-S5 fell off, indicating that the mechanical strength of MoS2@TCN-S5 is very low. MoS2@TCN-S3 has a higher TC degradation performance than MoS2@TCN-S1 and 2, and after 15 cycles of experiments, the TC degradation rate of P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) only decreased by 4.5 % (Fig. S8), multiple cycles do not damage the structure of MoS2@TCN-S3 and do not greatly affect its degradation effect, which proves that MoS2@TCN-S3 has excellent mechanical strength and recycling practicability. Therefore, it is determined that 1:5:720 (MoS2@TCN-S3) is the best ratio of WPU, deionized water and MoS2@TCN.

The degradation performance of TC in different reaction systems is investigated when the initial pH of the solution is 7, and the reaction kinetics is fitted. It can be seen from Fig. 4e that, the four systems of PMS, Vis/PMS, PMS(TCN-S) and PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) have basically the same TC degradation performance, and the degradation rates are 31.8 %, 34.8 %, 32.8 % and 33.5 %, respectively. The degradation performance of P-SF-PMS(TCN-S) and P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) systems can be increased to 74.3 % and 94.3 %, respectively. The above results indicate that the activation of PMS can be accelerated only when visible light and catalyst are present together. In this case, MoS2@TCN-S3 produces photogenerated-e− and h+ under visible light irradiation, and further activate PMS, destroy the peroxide bond of PMS and produce ROS such as OH, SO4 − and SO5 − (Eqs. (1), (2) and (3). The kinetic constants of TC degradation in PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3), P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) and P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) systems are 0.0035, 0.0125 and 0.0233 min−1 (Fig. 4f), respectively, the calculated synergistic factor (SF) of PMS and P-SF system is 1.46 (

Fig. S9 shows the dark adsorption experiment of the prepared catalyst. It can be seen that MoS2 has a certain adsorption performance, while TCN-S and MoS2@TCN-S3 has almost no adsorption performance, this is because the load of MoS2 in MoS2@TCN is so low that it cannot play a strong adsorption role, so the mass transfer limitation has no effect on the removal reaction TC. After removing the adsorption rate, the removal rates of TC by PMS(MoS2) and P-SF-PMS(MoS2) system are 18.6 % and 19.3 % (Fig. S10), which is lower than the TC degradation rate when only PMS existed (31.8 %), and after turning on the xenon lamp, the removal rate of TC by MoS2 in P-SF system is only 3.6 %, indicating that, as a co-catalyst MoS2 could not alone exert photocatalytic activity or activate PMS. In addition, whether under the condition of P-SF or P-SF-PMS, MoS2@TCN-S3 shows higher TC degradation rate than MoS2 and TCN-S, this is because the direct Z-scheme heterojunction in MoS2@TCN accelerates the transfer of photogenerated-e− from TCN to MoS2, which promotes the separation of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs, and finally forms e− aggregation on the CB of MoS2 and h+ aggregation on the VB of TCN. These e− and h+ improve the redox ability of the catalyst [52], promote MoS2@TCN-S3 produce more H2O2 and ROS, and accelerate PMS activation and TC degradation. The mineralization rate was used as the index to further evaluate the degradation performance of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system (Fig. S11). The results show that after the reaction of 120 min, the mineralization rate of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system reaches 58.5 %, which is 2.8, 1.7 and 1.3 times that of P-SF (TCN-S), P-SF-PMS(TCN-S3) and P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) systems of TC mineralization. MoS2@TCN-S3 can use less PMS to achieve better degradation of TC, and its degradation effect is even higher than that of some reported powder photocatalysts (Table S1).

The stability of P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system is analyzed by cyclic stability experiments and metal ion leaching experiments. After 15 cyclic experiments, the TC degradation rates of P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system slightly decrease from 94.3 % to 89.6 % (Fig. 4g), with a total decrease of less than 5 %, indicating that the P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system has excellent performance stability. The leaching concentration of Mo4+ during TC degradation in P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system was determined by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Text S3). The highest leaching concentration of Mo4+ in 15 consecutive cycles is only 0.17 mg/L (Fig. S12), and there is almost no Mo4+ leaching of MoS2@TCN-S3 after 15 cycles, indicating that P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system has good stability and will not cause secondary pollution. The above results show that P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system has strong cycle performance and stability, and has good practical application potential.

The process of adsorption and activation of PMS on the surface of MoS2, TCN and MoS2@TCN are studied by DFT to deeply analyze the transformation process of PMS to form SO4 − or OH in P-SF-PMS system. MoS2 (Fig. S13) and TCN (Fig. 5 a–c) will adsorb the O atom of PMS and transfer photogenerated-e− to PMS to make the O-O bond break to form a transition state, and then further form SO4 − or OH, and on MoS2@TCN, PMS will be adsorbed on the surface of MoS2 (Fig. 5d–f). The adsorption energy of MoS2@TCN (-3.52 eV, Fig. S14) for PMS is stronger than that of MoS2 (-0.83 eV) and TCN (-1.70 eV), so it is easier for MoS2@TCN to adsorb PMS and decompose it. By comparing the differential charge of PMS adsorbed on the surface of TCN and MoS2@TCN (Fig. 5g, h), it is found that when PMS is adsorbed by the catalyst, an obvious net electron accumulation region is formed around the O atom close to the catalyst on PMS, while a net electron depletion region is formed on the surface of the catalyst, indicating that the photogenerated-e− on the catalyst surface can be transferred to PMS through the O atom close to the catalyst on PMS, then it is further diffused to other atoms of PMS and breaks O-O to form SO4 − or OH. It is worth noting that, compared with TCN, the electron depletion region formed on the MoS2@TCN surface and the net electron accumulation region formed on the PMS surface after MoS2@TCN adsorbs PMS are significantly larger. The results show that the direct Z-scheme heterojunction formed on MoS2@TCN accelerates the transfer of photogenerated-e− and promotes the transfer of more e− from MoS2@TCN to PMS, which is beneficial to the transformation of PMS to SO4 − or OH.

Fig. 5i shows the change of free energy during the activation of PMS by MoS2, TCN and MoS2@TCN to generate SO4 − and OH. In the reaction process, *+HSO5 will first be adsorbed by the catalyst and converted to *HSO5, then the O-O bond of *HSO5 will be broken, and *HSO5 will be transformed to the transition state (TS). This process belongs to the endothermic process, which needs to overcome a large energy level barrier, so it is the rate-controlled process of the whole reaction. The conversion of *HSO5 to transition state by MoS2 and TCN needs to overcome the energy level barrier of 3.76 and 1.88 eV, while the conversion of *HSO5 to transition state by MoS2@TCN only needs to overcome the energy level barrier of 0.43 eV. The construction of Z-scheme heterojunction between TCN and MoS2 significantly reduces the energy barrier during the transition from *HSO5 to transition state, which accelerates the transformation from *+HSO5 to SO4 − and OH, which is the decisive factor that MoS2@TCN can produce more SO4 − and OH. In summary, compared with TCN, direct Z-scheme heterojunction enables MoS2@TCN to exhibit faster electron transfer ability, faster H2O2 generation ability, stronger adsorption capacity for PMS, and lower activation barrier for *HSO5.

The effects of different reaction conditions on the degradation of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system are explored. It shows that the degradation performance of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) increased with the increase of light intensity and PMS addition, but the rate of increase has gradually slowed. With the increase of the amount of catalyst, the degradation performance of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) increases at first and then decreases, because too many catalysts will hinder the penetration of light in the reactor. When the concentration of TC increases from 10 to 20 mg/L, the degradation effect of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system gradually weakened, and because the lower concentration of pollutants is difficult to be captured by ROS in the system, when the concentration of TC is 5 mg/L the degradation effect of P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) is slightly lower than that of 10 mg/L. Temperature is also an important factor affecting the P-SF-PMS system. With the increase of temperature, the degradation rate of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system increases gradually. When the temperature is 35 °C, P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) can degrade 97.6 % of TC in just 40 min. A detailed analysis of the effects of different reaction conditions on the P-SF-PMS system is shown in Text S7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS mutual activation mechanism in P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system
ce:section.ce:para:
The initial pH values of P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) and P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) systems are adjusted by HCl or NaOH to investigate the effect of the pH values on the TC degradation performance of different oxidation systems. In order to deeply analyze the changes of degradation performance of different oxidation systems under different pH conditions, methanol (MeOH, SO4 − and OH quenchers), tert-butanol (TBA, OH quencher), p-benzoquinone (BQ, O2 – quencher), furfuryl alcohol (FFA, 1O2 quencher), triethanolamine (TEOA, h+ quencher) and K2Cr2O7 (e− quencher) are used as quenchers for quenching experiments, the contribution of each active substance in the degradation process of TC by different oxidation systems is calculated via Eq. (4),

where, R(x) represents the contribution of active substance to the degradation kinetics, %; k(No) represents the reaction rate constant without quencher, min−1; k(x) represents the reaction rate constant after adding quencher, min−1.

Quenching experiments are carried out with BQ, K2Cr2O7 and TEOA to study the effects of different active species in the process of in situ formation of H2O2 in P-SF system driven by MoS2@TCN-S3 when pH is 7. It is found that the H2O2 production in P-SF system decreased by 88.00 % and 80.44 % respectively after the addition of BQ and K2Cr2O7 (Fig. 6 a), indicating that the P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system mainly depends on the role of O2 – and e− to generate H2O2. The output of H2O2 in P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system decreased by 99.0 % after the introduction of N2, indicating that the production of H2O2 is inseparable from the participation of dissolved oxygen. From this it can be inferred that the photocatalytic production of H2O2 follows the two electron O2 reduction reaction pathway [53]. Specifically, O2 first generates O2 – under the action of e− (Eq. (5), and O2 – continues to generate H2O2 together with H+ (Eq. (6). When the pH of the solution is 3, 5, 7, and 9, with the decrease of pH, the yield of H2O2 in the P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) decreases gradually (Fig. 6b), this is because acidic conditions will provide more H+ to promote the generation of H2O2, and under alkaline conditions, H2O2 is unstable and easy to be consumed (Eqs. (7) and (8). The degradation rate of TC by P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system is 83.3 %, 80.7 %, 78.1 and 57.7 % when the pH of the solution is 3, 5, 7, and 9 (Fig. 6c), which also decreases with the increases of pH. The above results confirm that the degradation performance of P-SF system driven by MoS2@TCN-S3 on TC is positively correlated with H2O2 production [54]. The adsorption performance of MoS2@TCN-S3 on TC is almost unaffected by pH changes (Fig. S15a). Because the zero-point charge (pHzpc) on the surface of MoS2@TCN is 4.5 (Fig. S15b), and TC is an amphoteric compound (which it produces cations when pH is less than 3.3, produces zwitterion species when pH is 3.3 ∼ 7.7, and produces anions when pH is greater than 7.7 [5]), the adsorption effect of MoS2@TCN-S3 to TC is very little affected by pH.

In the P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system, e− and O2 – play a major role in promoting the production of H2O2 (Eq. (6) and the activation of OH (Eqs. (9) and (10) (Fig. 6d, Fig. S16), while O2 – reacts with h+ to generate 1O2 (Eq. (11). When the initial pH of the solution is acidic or neutral, the change of TC degradation rate by P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system (Fig. 6c) is basically consistent with the change of H2O2 yield and R(OH), indicating that the degradation of TC by P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system depends on the activation of H2O2. When the initial pH of the solution changed from neutral to alkaline, the decrease of TC degradation rate of P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) system is much larger than that of H2O2 yield, and the decrease of TC degradability is no longer consistent with the decrease of H2O2 yield. At this time, R(OH) also decreases dramatically, it shows that the activation of H2O2 is inhibited, which is the main reason for the decline of TC degradation in P-SF system under alkaline condition. It can be seen that the degradation of TC in P-SF system mainly depends on the activation of H2O2 by MoS2@TCN-S3. The detailed analysis of ROS generation mechanism in P-SF system is shown in Text S8.

The ineffective activation of H2O2 is the reason why the P-SF oxidation system driven by MoS2@TCN-S3 cannot rapidly degrade TC under alkaline conditions. Adding PMS to the P-SF oxidation system to construct a P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system, and setting the initial pH of the solution to 9, surprisingly, the degradation rate of TC by the P-SF-PMS system is significantly accelerated, and the degradation rate of TC can reach 81.2 % in only 5 min (Fig. 6e), and the final degradation rate increased by 38.3 %. After the end of the reaction, the residual amount of H2O2 in P-SF-PMS system is only 7.6 μmol·g−1·h−1 (Fig. S17a), a large amount of H2O2 is consumed in P-SF-PMS system. It shows that H2O2 in P-SF-PMS system has synergistic interaction with PMS to generate more ROS, which is the key to improve the performance of P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system under alkaline condition. The quenching experiment showed that R(e−) = 86.8 % > R(1O2) = 85.7 % > R(SO4 − and OH) = 60.7 % > R(O2 –) = 58.4 % > R(OH) = 55.8 % > R(h+) = 15.8 % in P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system at pH = 9 (Fig. 6f, Fig. S18). Compared with P-SF system, R(e−), R(1O2) and R(OH) of P-SF-PMS system increased by 11.2 %, 48.2 % and 49.19 %, indicating that 1O2 and OH played an important role in improving the degradation performance of P-SF-PMS system under alkaline conditions. Under alkaline conditions and under the action of H2O2 generated by the P-SF system driven by MoS2@TCN-S3, PMS will be quickly converted into long-lived 1O2 (Eq. (12). In addition, PMS and HO2 – can also be converted to SO4 − under the activation of photogenerated-e− (Eq. (13), and then further converted to OH under alkaline conditions (Eq. (14). The mutual activation mechanism of P-SF system and PMS is the fundamental reason for the substantial increase of R(1O2) and R(OH) in the P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system, and also the main reason for the improvement of its TC degradation performance. Higher pH will also provide more OH– to promote the conversion of SO5 − generated by Eq. (3) to OH with higher oxidation performance (Eq. (15) [51], which is one of the reasons for the improvement of P-SF-PMS performance under alkaline conditions. It is worth noting that PMS can also be activated by alkali and self-decomposition to produce 1O2 (Eq. (16) [55], but the degradation rate of TC by PMS under alkaline condition is only 7.8 % higher than that under neutral condition (Fig. S19), so the activation of PMS in alkaline condition is not the decisive reason that P-SF-PMS system has stronger degradation performance to TC.

The quenching experiment investigated the contribution of different active substances to the P-SF-PMS multi-functional oxidation system driven by MoS2@TCN-S3 under acidic and alkaline conditions. It is found that when the solution pH changed from 9 to 3, R(e−), R(SO4 − and OH), R(O2 –) and R(h+) in P-SF-PMS system increased by 6.2 %, 10.8 %, 25.0 % and 14.7 %, respectively. While R(1O2) and R(OH) decreased by 27.6 % and 32.8 %. In the P-SF-PMS system driven by MoS2@TCN-S3, the degradability of TC decreased with the decrease of pH value. This is because the reaction shown by Eqs.12–15 are gradually inhibited with the decrease of pH value of the solution, so that R (1O2) and R (OH) also show a decreasing trend. It is worth noting that under acidic and neutral conditions, the degradation performance of TC in the P-SF-PMS system driven by MoS2@TCN-S3 is basically the same, which is different from that of the P-SF system driven by MoS2@TCN-S3, indicating that the P-SF-PMS system driven by MoS2@TCN-S3 has a stronger adaptability to pH value. With the decrease of pH in solution, R(O2 –) increases gradually. When the solution is acidic and neutral, O2 – becomes the main ROS in P-SF-PMS system, which dominates the degradation of TC by MoS2@TCN-S3. At this time, the oxidation ability of h+ is enhanced, and more h+ can directly oxidize and degrade TC, which explains why R(h+) is higher under acidic conditions. With the increase of R(h+), the amount of h+ on the surface of MoS2@TCN-S3 decreases gradually, and the recombination rate of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs decrease, so there are more photogenerated-e− can participate in the formation of O2 – and the mutual activation process of PMS and H2O2, and R(e−) will be higher under acidic condition. With the decrease of pH in solution, R(OH) also decreases, indicating that the degradation of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system is no longer depends on the activation of H2O2. Without the alkaline condition, the SO4 − generated by the mutual activation of PMS and H2O2 (Eq. (13) is no longer converted into OH by the reaction Eq. (14), while O2 – will also activate PMS to form new SO4 − (Eq. (17). Therefore, with the decrease of solution pH, R(SO4 − and OH) is in an upward trend. To sum up, the degradation of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) multifunctional oxidation system mainly depends on O2 – under acidic and neutral conditions, which overcomes the dependence of PSF system on H+.

The contents of H2O2 and PMS in P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system are determined after the reaction (the concentration of PMS is determined by ABTS method [56], and the detection method is described in Text S5). The content of H2O2 in P-SF-PMS is much lower than that in P-SF system when pH is 3, 5, 7 and 9 (Fig. S17a), indicating that the utilization rate of H2O2 in P-SF-PMS system is increased. Under the condition that the initial pH of the solution is 3, 5, 7 and 9, after 120 min reaction, the PMS content in the P-SF-PMS system decreased by about 95 % (Fig. S17b), indicating that most of the PMS in the P-SF-PMS system is utilized, which is beneficial to improve the degradation performance of the P-SF-PMS system and also reduce the impact of PMS residues on the environment. The above data once again prove that there is a synergistic effect between H2O2 and PMS in the P-SF-PMS system, which jointly promote the degradation of TC by MoS2@TCN-S3.

The strength of ROS generated in P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) and P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) systems is compared by ESR test. The results showed that P-SF(MoS2@TCN-S3) generated O2 –, 1O2 and OH after illumination, and P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) generated O2 –, 1O2, OH and SO4 −. After 30 min illumination, the signal intensity of O2 – in P-SF-PMS system is 5.48 times that of P-SF system (Fig. 6g), the signal intensity of 1O2 is 8.26 times that of P-SF system (Fig. 6h), and the signal intensity of OH is 10.90 times that P-SF system (Fig. 6i). Compare with the P-SF system, the strength of each ROS in the P-SF-PMS system is greatly enhanced, which is consistent with the results of quenching experiments.

So far, we can infer the synergistic effect of P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system and the mutual activation mechanism of ROS in the process of TC degradation by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system (Scheme 1 ): MoS2@TCN-S3 produces photogenerated e− and h+ under visible light irradiation (Eq. (18), due to the existence of direct Z-scheme heterojunction between TCN and MoS2, the photogenerated-e− on the TCN surface will transfer to the VB of MoS2 and combine with the h+, thus preventing the recombination of photogenerated-e− and h+ on the MoS2 surface. The photogenerated-e− on the MoS2 CB can reduce O2 to O2 – to further form H2O2 and accelerate the activation of PMS to generate more ROS. The photogenerated-h+ on the VB of TCN can activate PMS to form SO5 − (these SO5 − will be converted into OH under alkaline conditions by the action of OH–). Under alkaline conditions, under the action of photogenerated-e−, PMS and H2O2 can activate each other to form SO4 −, and further form OH. PMS can also generate 1O2 through activation by H2O2 (in situ generated by MoS2@TCN-S driven P-SF system) and alkali activation. Finally, TC is degraded to CO2 and H2O under the action of a large amount of 1O2 and OH, the remaining SO4 − and O2 – (Eq. (19) and the h+ on the TCN VB. Thanks to the synergistic effect of P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system, it promotes the formation of 1O2 and OH, and P-SF-PMS system overcomes the problem of poor performance under alkaline conditions. Under acidic and neutral conditions, O2 – becomes the main ROS in the P-SF-PMS system, and the direct oxidation ability of h+ to TC is enhanced, which promotes the separation of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs, so that more e− can participate in the generation of ROS such as O2 – and SO4 −. Under the guidance of O2 –, PMS is accelerated to activate and produce a large number of SO4 −. Finally, P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) multifunctional oxidation system can achieve efficient degradation of TC without relying on the activation of H2O2 under acidic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation pathway of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze the degradation pathway of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system, the intermediates in the process of TC degradation are analyzed by DFT calculation and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). Fig. 7 a–c shows the chemical structure, highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of TC. Due to the weak binding between e− and HOMO, HOMO is vulnerable to attack by free radicals in the reaction [57]. The active sites of free radical attack (f(r)), nucleophilic attack (f(+)) and electrophilic attack (f(-)) of TC are identified by Fukui index (Fig. 7d) [58]. Combined with the HOMO and Fukui index of TC, it is inferred that C (7), C (14), O (20), O (21) and N (28) are more likely to be attacked. It can be seen from the corresponding mass spectrum that there is a product with a mass-nucleus ratio (m/z) of 445, 430, 416, 414, 318, 305, 276, 274, 244, 230, 200, 150, 128 and 122 in the process of TC degradation by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system (Fig. S20, Table S2), in which the product with m/z = 445 is TC. According to the measured intermediates, and the previous research reports, two degradation pathways of TC are analyzed (Fig. 7e). In pathway I, firstly, TC removes two methyl groups and carbonylated to form R1 (m/z = 416) under the action of ROS [59], then a ring of R1 is broken and R2 (m/z = 305) is generated by deaminated and demethylated of R1, R2 continues to remove amino and carbonyl groups to form R3 (m/z = 276), R3 is dehydrated and demethylated to form R4 (m/z = 230), while R4 continued to react to form R5 (m/z = 200). R5 is decomposed to form R6 (m/z = 150), R7 (m/z = 122) or R8 (m/z = 128) and eventually mineralized into CO2 and H2O. In pathway Ⅱ, in the action of ROS, TC is demethylated to form R9 (m/z = 430), then dehydroxylated to R10 (m/z = 414), R10 is deaminated and demethylated to form R11 (m/z = 318), R11 removes a propyl group to form R12 (m/z = 274) [60], R13 (m/z = 244) is formed by R12 removing formyl group, R14 (m/z = 200) is formed after the carbonyl group of R13 is destroyed and hydroxymethyl is removed. The structure of R14 is further destroyed and completely mineralized to form CO2 and H2O after the formation of R6 (m/z = 150), R15 (m/z = 122) or R16 (m/z = 128) [61,62]. The TOC removal rate of TC in P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system is 65.7 % (Fig. S21), which is 26.2 % higher than that in the P-SF system. The higher TOC removal rate indicates that the P-SF-PMS system can degrade TC more thoroughly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application performance of P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system
ce:section.ce:para:
In practical application, sewage often contains many inorganic ions, and their existence may interfere with the operation of P-SF-PMS system. NaCl (Cl−), NaNO3 (NO3 –), NaSO4 (SO4 2−), NaHCO3 (HCO3 –) and NaH2PO4 (HPO4 2−) are used to determine the effect of interference anions on the degradation of TC in P-SF-PMS system. After adding Cl−, NO3 –, SO4 2−, HCO3 – and HPO4 2− the degradation rates of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system are 96.2 %, 93.7 %, 95.2 %, 94.6 % and 82.9 % respectively (Fig. 8 a). It can be seen that the addition of Cl− slightly accelerates the rate of degradation reaction of TC, because Cl− reacts with PMS to form active chlorine (Eq. (20) [11]. The addition of NO3 – and SO4 2− had little effect on the degradation reaction. After adding HCO3 –, the degradation rate of TC by MoS2@TCN-S3 is greatly improved, and 87.5 % of TC is degraded only after 20 min reaction, this is because the presence of HCO3 – will increase the pH of the solution to accelerate the formation of 1O2 and OH. The degradation rate of TC in the system decreased significantly after the addition of HPO4 2−, which may be due to the quenching of SO4 − and OH by HPO4 2− (Eqs. (21), (22) and (23) [63]. CaSO4 (Ca2+), MgSO4 (Mg2+) and ZnSO4 (Zn2+) are used to explore the effect of interference cations on the degradation performance of P-SF-PMS system. The degradation rates of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) under the influence of Ca2+, Mg2+ and Zn2+ are 93.6 %, 91.0 % and 94.8 % (Fig. 8b), all interference cations have little effect on the degradation of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system. The above results show that the P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system can resist the interference of most common inorganic ions.

The treatment effect of P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system on domestic sewage (DS) and secondary sedimentation tank effluent of domestic sewage (DS-SE) is analyzed by 3D fluorescence spectrum, and the removal performance of chemical oxygen demand (COD) is investigated. The water quality of the two kinds of wastewater is shown in Table S3. DS (Fig. 8c) and DS-SE (Fig. 8d) mainly include A (aromatic protein, λex/em = 250/370), B (fulvic acid and fulvic acid-like acid, λex/em = 250/455,) and C (tryptophan and protein-like, λex/em = 275/330) and D (humic acid polymer, λex/em = 300/455). During the reaction, the fluorescence intensity of component B and D decrease most rapidly, while the fluorescence intensity of component A increases at first and then decreases. This is because the components B and D of macromolecules are easily decomposed by MoS2@TCN-S3 into aromatic substances of small molecules, resulting in the gradual accumulation of component A in the P-SF-PMS system, and these aromatic substances have a robust π structure, which is difficult to be completely degraded in a short time. After 240 min, the fluorescence intensity of component A of DS and DS-SE decrease by 51.2 % and 43.2 %. The fluorescence intensity of component C decrease by 84.6 % and 82.6 %. The COD removal rates of DS and DS-SE by P-SF-PMS system are 64.6 % and 58.7 % (Fig. 8e), which are 1.8 and 1.9 times higher than that of P-SF system. The above data show that the P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system has good potential to treat practical wastewater.

In practical applications, xenon lamp consumes a large amount of energy, and the volume of the reactor is limited. As the most commonly used and cheapest energy source in the field of wastewater purification [64], if natural light can be used to degrade pollutants, it can reduce energy consumption and increase the amount of wastewater treatment. Therefore, the degradation of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system under natural light is tested, and the test method and testing device are shown in Text S9 and Fig. S22. The degradation rate of TC by P-SF-PMS(MoS2@TCN-S3) system is 77.9 % (Fig. 8f), which is 34.9 % higher than that in P-SF system. These results show that MoS2@TCN-S3 can also drive P-SF-PMS system under natural light and exhibit good TC degradation performance. Because the light intensity cannot be controlled under natural light intensity, five cycle experiments are carried out to analyze the stability of P-SF-PMS system under natural light (Fig. S23, all the other influencing factors except light are the same in five experiments). In the five cycle experiments, the degradation rate of P-SF-PMS system to TC under natural light is maintained between 76.5 % and 84.4 %, indicating that the degradation performance of P-SF-PMS system is little affected by the change of natural light, and its degradation performance of TC can be maintained at about 80 %. The effects of different PMS addition, catalyst dosage and pollutant concentration on the P-SF-PMS system under natural light are compared, and the results are basically consistent with the experimental results under simulated light (Fig. S24a-c), and the P-SF-PMS system still maintains good pH applicability under natural light (Fig. S24d). The above data prove that the P-SF-PMS multifunctional oxidation system has good practical application potential under the condition of natural light.

In summary, we have successfully prepared the MoS2@carbon nitride nanotube composite photocatalytic hollow spheres (MoS2@TCN-S), which has good cycling practicability. There is a good mutual activation mechanism of ROS between P-SF and PMS in P-SF-PMS multi-functional oxidation system: under alkaline conditions, PMS and H2O2 generated on MoS2@TCN-S activate each other and form 1O2 and OH, which solves the problem of poor degradation of P-SF system under alkaline conditions; under acidic and neutral conditions, the activation of PMS by O2 – and the synergistic effect of PMS and H2O2 make the P-SF-PMS system get rid of the dependence on H+. This synergistic mechanism between P-SF and PMS makes P-SF-PMS system have good TC degradation performance and extensive pH adaptability, but this mechanism has not been studied in previous reports. P-SF-PMS system also has good practical wastewater treatment capacity and natural light response ability. This study provides a green and sustainable photocatalyst for the removal of water pollutants, and has a good potential for practical application.
